title: Rhabdomyolysis
targetAudience: Medical Students, Residents, Emergency Medicine, Nephrology, Critical Care
objectives: Recognize clinical presentation and common causes
Manage with aggressive IV fluid therapy

vignette.text:
A 32-year-old man presents with severe bilateral thigh pain, weakness, and dark urine after completing his first marathon yesterday. He reports muscle cramping during the race and minimal fluid intake.

Past Medical History: None

Medications: None

Allergies: NKDA

physicalExam.musculoskeletal: Bilateral thigh tenderness, mild swelling, pain with passive stretch

questions.stem: This patient has muscle pain, dark urine, and CK 45,000 after exertion.
questions.question: What defines rhabdomyolysis and how does it present?

options.text: CK >500 U/L defines rhabdomyolysis
Dark urine is always present

RHABDOMYOLYSIS DEFINITION: Skeletal muscle breakdown with release of intracellular contents into bloodstream; CK elevation is hallmark - typically >5x upper limit of normal (>1000 U/L); CLASSIC TRIAD (present in <10%): Myalgia; Weakness; Dark urine (tea/cola colored); CLINICAL FEATURES: Muscle pain, tenderness, swelling; Weakness; Dark urine (myoglobinuria); Fever, malaise; May be asymptomatic with incidental CK elevation; LABORATORY FINDINGS: CK: Markedly elevated (can be >100,000); Peaks 24-72h, t½ ~48h; Myoglobin: Elevated (causes dark urine); Clears faster than CK; Urine dipstick: + blood but - RBCs on microscopy; Electrolytes: ↑K+, ↑Phosphate, ↓Ca2+ (early), ↑Ca2+ (late)
Classic triad present in most cases

questions.explanation: Rhabdomyolysis Recognition: DEFINITION: Skeletal muscle breakdown releasing intracellular contents. CK >5x ULN (typically >1000 U/L). CLASSIC TRIAD (present in <10%): Myalgia, weakness, dark urine. CLINICAL: Muscle pain/tenderness/swelling, weakness, cola-colored urine, fever. LABS: CK markedly elevated (peaks 24-72h), urine dipstick positive for blood but minimal RBCs on microscopy (myoglobin cross-reacts), hyperkalemia, hyperphosphatemia, hypocalcemia early.

sources.text: StatPearls: Rhabdomyolysis. NCBI Bookshelf. Accessed January 14, 2026.

questions.stem: You're determining the cause of his rhabdomyolysis.
questions.question: What are the common causes of rhabdomyolysis?

options.text: Exercise-induced is always benign
Infections never cause rhabdomyolysis

RHABDOMYOLYSIS CAUSES: TRAUMATIC/PHYSICAL: Crush injury, compartment syndrome; Intense exercise (marathon, CrossFit, military training); Immobilization/prolonged pressure; Seizures, status epilepticus; Electrical injury, lightning; DRUGS/TOXINS: Alcohol (common); Statins (especially with interacting drugs); Cocaine, amphetamines, MDMA; Heroin (often with immobilization); NMS, serotonin syndrome, malignant hyperthermia; METABOLIC/ENDOCRINE: Hypokalemia, hypophosphatemia; Hypothyroidism, DKA; INFECTIONS: Influenza, COVID-19, Legionella; GENETIC: McArdle disease, CPT II deficiency; This patient: EXERTIONAL - marathon + dehydration
Statins are the most common cause

questions.explanation: Rhabdomyolysis Causes: TRAUMATIC/PHYSICAL: Crush injury, compartment syndrome, intense exercise, immobilization, seizures. DRUGS/TOXINS: Alcohol (common), statins, cocaine/amphetamines/MDMA, heroin (with immobilization), NMS/serotonin syndrome/malignant hyperthermia. METABOLIC: Hypokalemia, hypophosphatemia, hypothyroidism, DKA. INFECTIONS: Influenza, COVID-19, Legionella. GENETIC: McArdle disease, CPT II deficiency (consider if recurrent). This patient: Exertional from marathon with dehydration.

sources.text: StatPearls: Rhabdomyolysis. NCBI Bookshelf. Accessed January 14, 2026.

questions.stem: You're explaining the mechanism of injury.
questions.question: What is the pathophysiology of rhabdomyolysis and its complications?

options.text: Alkaline urine worsens kidney injury

PATHOPHYSIOLOGY: Muscle cell membrane damage → Loss of ATP-dependent ion pumps → Calcium influx → Myocyte death → Release of intracellular contents; RELEASED CONTENTS: Myoglobin → Filtered by kidney → Toxic to tubules; CK, LDH, AST, aldolase; Potassium → Life-threatening hyperkalemia; Phosphate → Binds calcium; Purines → Converted to uric acid; AKI MECHANISMS: (1) Renal vasoconstriction (myoglobin scavenges NO); (2) Direct tubular toxicity (myoglobin); (3) Tubular obstruction (myoglobin casts); (4) Exacerbated by: Acidic urine, hypovolemia; ELECTROLYTE ABNORMALITIES: Hyperkalemia (cell lysis) - DANGEROUS; Hyperphosphatemia; Hypocalcemia (early) - phosphate binding; Hypercalcemia (late) - calcium mobilization from damaged muscle
Hypocalcemia is a late finding
Myoglobin is protective to the kidney

questions.explanation: Rhabdomyolysis Pathophysiology: Muscle membrane damage → ATP depletion → calcium influx → myocyte death → release of contents. RELEASED CONTENTS: Myoglobin (nephrotoxic), CK, potassium, phosphate, purines. AKI MECHANISMS: Renal vasoconstriction, direct tubular toxicity, tubular obstruction by casts - all worsened by acidic urine and hypovolemia. ELECTROLYTES: Hyperkalemia (dangerous), hyperphosphatemia, hypocalcemia early (phosphate binding), hypercalcemia late (mobilization from muscle).

sources.text: StatPearls: Rhabdomyolysis. NCBI Bookshelf. Accessed January 14, 2026.

questions.stem: This patient has CK 45,000 and creatinine 1.9.
questions.question: How do you risk stratify for AKI in rhabdomyolysis?

options.text: Hydration status doesn't affect AKI risk
CK >5000 always causes AKI
Age is not a risk factor

AKI RISK FACTORS: Higher CK: Risk increases above 15,000-20,000 U/L; CK >40,000 = high risk (~50% develop AKI); Dehydration/hypovolemia; Pre-existing kidney disease; Acidosis (enhances myoglobin toxicity); Sepsis; Older age; Concurrent nephrotoxins (NSAIDs, contrast); RISK PREDICTION SCORES: McMahon Score variables: Age, sex, initial Cr, initial CK; Calcium, phosphate, bicarbonate; Score ≥6 = high risk for death or RRT; PROTECTIVE FACTORS: Early aggressive fluid resuscitation; Younger age; Single etiology (vs multiple insults); This patient: CK 45,000 + AKI + dehydration = HIGH RISK

questions.explanation: AKI Risk Stratification: RISK FACTORS: CK level (risk increases >15,000-20,000, high risk >40,000), dehydration, pre-existing CKD, acidosis, sepsis, older age, nephrotoxins. McMahon Score predicts death/RRT using age, sex, creatinine, CK, calcium, phosphate, bicarbonate - score ≥6 is high risk. This patient: CK 45,000 + already elevated creatinine + dehydration = HIGH RISK requiring aggressive management.

questions.question: What is the management of rhabdomyolysis?

options.text: RHABDOMYOLYSIS MANAGEMENT: IV FLUIDS (cornerstone): Aggressive isotonic crystalloid (NS or LR); Initial: 1-2 L/hr until euvolemic; Then: 200-500 mL/hr to maintain UOP 200-300 mL/hr; Goal: UOP 3 mL/kg/hr; Monitor for fluid overload; BICARBONATE (controversial): Theoretical benefit: Alkalinize urine → ↓myoglobin toxicity; NOT routinely recommended; Consider if: Severe acidosis, High CK (>40,000), Urine pH <6.5; Caution: May worsen hypocalcemia; MANNITOL (not routinely recommended): Osmotic diuresis; Limited evidence; May worsen AKI if hypovolemic; ELECTROLYTE MANAGEMENT: Hyperkalemia: Aggressive treatment (calcium, insulin/glucose, kayexalate, dialysis if severe); Hypocalcemia: DON'T replace unless symptomatic (will redistribute later); Hyperphosphatemia: Phosphate binders if severe
Fluid restriction is recommended
Mannitol is first-line therapy
Always replace calcium aggressively

questions.explanation: Rhabdomyolysis Management: IV FLUIDS are cornerstone - aggressive isotonic crystalloid (1-2 L/hr initially, then 200-500 mL/hr) targeting UOP 200-300 mL/hr. BICARBONATE is controversial, not routinely recommended but may consider for severe acidosis or very high CK. ELECTROLYTES: Treat hyperkalemia aggressively, but DON'T replace calcium unless symptomatic (will normalize as muscle heals). Monitor closely for fluid overload.

sources.text: StatPearls: Rhabdomyolysis. NCBI Bookshelf. Accessed January 14, 2026.

correct.reinforcement: Correct! questions.question: How should hyperkalemia be managed in rhabdomyolysis?

options.text: Calcium gluconate lowers potassium
ECG monitoring is not needed
Dialysis is first-line for K+ 5.8

HYPERKALEMIA IN RHABDOMYOLYSIS: SEVERITY ASSESSMENT: K+ 5.5-6.0: Mild; K+ 6.0-6.5: Moderate; K+ >6.5 or ECG changes: Severe; ECG CHANGES (in order): Peaked T waves → PR prolongation → QRS widening → Sine wave → VF/asystole; TREATMENT: MEMBRANE STABILIZATION (if ECG changes): Calcium gluconate 1-2g IV over 5-10 min; Does NOT lower K+, protects heart; SHIFT K+ INTO CELLS: Insulin 10 units + D50W (onset 15-30 min); Albuterol nebulizer (onset 30 min); Sodium bicarbonate (if acidotic); REMOVE K+ FROM BODY: Furosemide (if volume tolerant); Kayexalate/patiromer (slow onset); HEMODIALYSIS: For refractory hyperkalemia; This patient: K+ 5.8 - moderate; Treat with insulin/glucose, monitor ECG, aggressive fluids

questions.explanation: Hyperkalemia Management: ASSESS SEVERITY with K+ level and ECG. MEMBRANE STABILIZATION with calcium gluconate if ECG changes (doesn't lower K+). SHIFT K+ INTO CELLS: Insulin + glucose, albuterol, bicarbonate if acidotic. REMOVE K+: Diuretics if volume tolerant, potassium binders, hemodialysis for refractory. This patient: K+ 5.8 is moderate - treat with insulin/glucose while aggressive fluids, continuous ECG monitoring.

questions.question: What are the major complications of rhabdomyolysis?

options.text: AKI is rare in rhabdomyolysis
Dialysis is never needed
Compartment syndrome is not associated

RHABDOMYOLYSIS COMPLICATIONS: ACUTE KIDNEY INJURY (10-50%): Pigment nephropathy; May require dialysis (10-15% of AKI); Usually recovers with supportive care; HYPERKALEMIA: Life-threatening arrhythmias; Requires aggressive monitoring and treatment; COMPARTMENT SYNDROME: Muscle swelling within fascial compartments; 5 P's: Pain (out of proportion), Pain with passive stretch, Paresthesias, Pulselessness, Paralysis; Requires emergent fasciotomy; DIC: From tissue factor release; CARDIAC ARRHYTHMIAS: From hyperkalemia, hypocalcemia; HEPATIC INJURY: Elevated transaminases (muscle source); TRUE liver injury possible from hypoperfusion; LATE HYPERCALCEMIA: As calcium remobilizes from damaged muscle

questions.explanation: Rhabdomyolysis Complications: AKI (10-50%) from pigment nephropathy, may need dialysis but usually recovers. HYPERKALEMIA causing life-threatening arrhythmias. COMPARTMENT SYNDROME (5 P's) requiring emergent fasciotomy. DIC from tissue factor release. Cardiac arrhythmias from electrolyte abnormalities. LATE HYPERCALCEMIA as calcium remobilizes from damaged muscle.

questions.question: What is the complete management approach?

options.text: Complete approach: (1) DIAGNOSIS: Classic presentation - muscle pain + dark urine + CK 45,000 after marathon; (2) ETIOLOGY: Exertional rhabdomyolysis with dehydration; (3) RISK ASSESSMENT: HIGH RISK - CK >40,000, AKI present (Cr 1.9), hyperkalemia (5.8); (4) AGGRESSIVE IV FLUIDS: NS or LR 1-2 L/hr initially; Target UOP 200-300 mL/hr (3 mL/kg/hr); May need 10+ L in first 24h; (5) TREAT HYPERKALEMIA: Insulin 10U + D50W; Continuous cardiac monitoring; Dialysis if refractory; (6) DON'T REPLACE CALCIUM unless symptomatic; (7) MONITOR: Serial CK (should peak 24-72h then decline); Serial creatinine, electrolytes; Urine output closely; Watch for compartment syndrome; (8) DIALYSIS if: Refractory hyperkalemia, Volume overload, Severe acidosis, Uremic symptoms
Restrict fluids to prevent overload
Immediately replace calcium
Single CK measurement is sufficient

questions.explanation: Complete Management: DIAGNOSIS confirmed by classic presentation. ETIOLOGY is exertional with dehydration. HIGH RISK given CK >40,000, AKI, hyperkalemia. TREATMENT: Aggressive IV fluids (1-2 L/hr targeting UOP 200-300 mL/hr), treat hyperkalemia with insulin/glucose and monitoring, DON'T replace calcium unless symptomatic. MONITOR: Serial CK (should peak 24-72h), creatinine, electrolytes, urine output, watch for compartment syndrome. DIALYSIS indications: refractory hyperkalemia, volume overload, severe acidosis, uremia.

sources.text: StatPearls: Rhabdomyolysis. NCBI Bookshelf. Accessed January 14, 2026.

mcqs.stem: Which of the following is the MOST important initial step in evaluating a patient with suspected rhabdomyolysis?

options.text: Order advanced imaging immediately
options.explanation: Advanced imaging may be indicated but should be guided by clinical findings, not ordered reflexively.

options.text: Consult a specialist before any workup
options.explanation: Initial evaluation should be performed by the treating physician; specialist consultation follows based on findings.

options.text: Start empiric treatment before evaluation
options.explanation: Treatment should generally follow an appropriate diagnostic evaluation unless the patient is critically unstable.

options.text: Obtain a thorough history and physical examination
options.explanation: A comprehensive history and physical examination is the foundation of clinical reasoning in rhabdomyolysis. It guides all subsequent diagnostic and management decisions.

correct.reinforcement: Correct! Obtain a thorough history and physical examination.
correct.deeperWhy: A comprehensive history and physical examination is the foundation of clinical reasoning in rhabdomyolysis. It guides all subsequent diagnostic and management decisions.
correct.clinicalPearl: A comprehensive history and physical examination is the foundation of clinical reasoning in rhabdomyolysis. It guides all subsequent diagnostic and management decisions.

A.misconception: Advanced imaging may be indicated but should be guided by clinical findings, not ordered reflexively.
A.guidance: Consider why Obtain a thorough history and physical examination is the key concept.
A.bridgeTo: The correct answer is Obtain a thorough history and physical examination because it best fits the clinical scenario.

B.misconception: Initial evaluation should be performed by the treating physician; specialist consultation follows based on findings.
B.guidance: Consider why Obtain a thorough history and physical examination is the key concept.
B.bridgeTo: The correct answer is Obtain a thorough history and physical examination because it best fits the clinical scenario.

mcqs.stem: A patient presents with classic features of rhabdomyolysis. Which clinical reasoning principle is MOST important in avoiding diagnostic error?

options.text: Order every available test to rule out all possibilities
options.explanation: Indiscriminate testing leads to false positives, unnecessary procedures, and increased cost without improving diagnostic accuracy.

options.text: Rely primarily on pattern recognition
options.explanation: While pattern recognition (System 1 thinking) is useful, it should be balanced with analytical reasoning (System 2) to avoid cognitive biases.

options.text: Search satisficing - stop looking after the first diagnosis
options.explanation: Premature closure (stopping the diagnostic process too early) is a common cognitive error that leads to missed diagnoses.

options.text: Consider the differential diagnosis systematically
options.explanation: Systematic consideration of differential diagnoses, including must-not-miss conditions, is essential for avoiding premature closure and anchoring bias.

correct.reinforcement: Correct! Consider the differential diagnosis systematically.
correct.deeperWhy: Systematic consideration of differential diagnoses, including must-not-miss conditions, is essential for avoiding premature closure and anchoring bias.
correct.clinicalPearl: Systematic consideration of differential diagnoses, including must-not-miss conditions, is essential for avoiding premature closure and anchoring bias.

A.guidance: Consider why Consider the differential diagnosis systematically is the key concept.
A.bridgeTo: The correct answer is Consider the differential diagnosis systematically because it best fits the clinical scenario.

B.misconception: While pattern recognition (System 1 thinking) is useful, it should be balanced with analytical reasoning (System 2) to avoid cognitive biases.
B.guidance: Consider why Consider the differential diagnosis systematically is the key concept.

C.guidance: Consider why Consider the differential diagnosis systematically is the key concept.

mcqs.stem: When managing rhabdomyolysis, which approach best supports evidence-based practice?

options.text: Follow institutional protocols regardless of patient factors
options.explanation: Evidence-based practice integrates best evidence with clinical expertise AND patient values/circumstances.

options.text: Always defer to the most recent published study
options.explanation: Individual studies should be appraised for quality and relevance; systematic reviews and guidelines generally provide stronger evidence.

options.text: Integrate current best evidence with clinical expertise and patient values
options.explanation: Evidence-based medicine is the conscientious integration of best research evidence, clinical expertise, and patient values in making clinical decisions.

options.text: Base decisions solely on clinical experience
options.explanation: Clinical experience is valuable but can introduce bias; it should be combined with current evidence.

correct.reinforcement: Correct! Integrate current best evidence with clinical expertise and patient values.
correct.deeperWhy: Evidence-based medicine is the conscientious integration of best research evidence, clinical expertise, and patient values in making clinical decisions.
correct.clinicalPearl: Evidence-based medicine is the conscientious integration of best research evidence, clinical expertise, and patient values in making clinical decisions.

mcqs.stem: A patient has the following ABG: pH 7.28, pCO2 24 mmHg, HCO3 11 mEq/L. Serum Na 140, Cl 100, K 4.0, albumin 4.0. What is the acid-base interpretation?

options.text: Non-anion gap metabolic acidosis (NAGMA) with appropriate respiratory compensation
options.explanation: Anion gap = Na - (Cl + HCO3) = 140 - (100 + 11) = 29. This is elevated (normal 12 ± 4), indicating an anion gap metabolic acidosis, not NAGMA.

options.text: Mixed anion gap metabolic acidosis and respiratory alkalosis
options.explanation: The pCO2 of 24 falls within Winter's formula range (22.5-26.5), indicating appropriate compensation rather than a primary respiratory alkalosis.

options.text: Anion gap metabolic acidosis with appropriate respiratory compensation
options.explanation: AG = 140 - (100+11) = 29 (elevated). Expected pCO2 by Winter's formula = 1.5(11) + 8 ± 2 = 22.5-26.5. Actual pCO2 of 24 is within this range, confirming appropriate respiratory compensation. Calculate delta-delta: Δ AG = 29-12 = 17; Δ HCO3 = 24-11 = 13. Ratio ~1.3 suggests pure AGMA without concurrent non-AG acidosis or metabolic alkalosis.

options.text: Anion gap metabolic acidosis with concurrent non-anion gap metabolic acidosis
options.explanation: Delta-delta ratio is ~1.3 (17/13), which falls in the 1-2 range indicating pure AGMA. A ratio <1 would suggest concurrent NAGMA.

correct.reinforcement: Correct! Anion gap metabolic acidosis with appropriate respiratory compensation.
correct.deeperWhy: AG = 140 - (100+11) = 29 (elevated). Expected pCO2 by Winter's formula = 1.5(11) + 8 ± 2 = 22.5-26.5. Actual pCO2 of 24 is within this range, confirming appropriate respiratory compensation. Calculate delta-delta: Δ AG = 29-12 = 17; Δ HCO3 = 24-11 = 13. Ratio ~1.3 suggests pure AGMA without concurre
correct.clinicalPearl: AG = 140 - (100+11) = 29 (elevated). Expected pCO2 by Winter's formula = 1.5(11) + 8 ± 2 = 22.5-26.5. Actual pCO2 of 24 is within this range, confirming appropriate respiratory compensation. Calculate

A.misconception: Anion gap = Na - (Cl + HCO3) = 140 - (100 + 11) = 29. This is elevated (normal 12 ± 4), indicating an anion gap metabolic acidosis, not NAGMA.
A.guidance: Consider why Anion gap metabolic acidosis with appropriate respiratory compensation is the key concept.
A.bridgeTo: The correct answer is Anion gap metabolic acidosis with appropriate respiratory compensation because it best fits the clinical scenario.

B.misconception: The pCO2 of 24 falls within Winter's formula range (22.5-26.5), indicating appropriate compensation rather than a primary respiratory alkalosis.
B.guidance: Consider why Anion gap metabolic acidosis with appropriate respiratory compensation is the key concept.
B.bridgeTo: The correct answer is Anion gap metabolic acidosis with appropriate respiratory compensation because it best fits the clinical scenario.

D.misconception: Delta-delta ratio is ~1.3 (17/13), which falls in the 1-2 range indicating pure AGMA. A ratio <1 would suggest concurrent NAGMA.
D.guidance: Consider why Anion gap metabolic acidosis with appropriate respiratory compensation is the key concept.
D.bridgeTo: The correct answer is Anion gap metabolic acidosis with appropriate respiratory compensation because it best fits the clinical scenario.
